topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun cast long, distorted shadows across the worn linoleum floor as I retrieved the mail. Every rustle of the envelope sounded amplified in the sudden silence of my apartment. There, tucked between a utility bill and a flyer for a garage sale, was a card. Unsigned. The paper felt strangely thick, almost like vellum. My fingers, already clammy, curled around the edges as I pulled it out. A cold prickle traced its way up my neck. Who would send this? No one had visited recently. I stared at the blank front, my breath catching in my throat. I quickly shoved it deep into my jacket pocket, the fabric rough against my palm. I kept glancing toward the window, half-expecting to see a figure standing on the street, watching.

[story 2]

I picked up the card with two fingers, my knuckles white against the stiff cardstock. The mailbox was just a standard metal box, yet holding this piece of paper felt like handling something illicit. My heart was hammering against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm I couldn't seem to slow down. It was addressed simply to ‘Someone.’ The handwriting on the inside, though faint, seemed deliberate, almost mocking. I immediately dropped it onto the kitchen counter, grabbed the nearest heavy book, and slammed it down over the spot, trying to smother the evidence. I paced the small living room, checking the locks twice, listening intently for any sound from the hallway, every creak sounding like a footstep approaching.

[story 3]

It was just a piece of cardstock, nothing more, yet the discovery sent a jolt of sheer dread through me. I picked it up under the harsh glare of the overhead light. The lack of an address, the absence of any recognizable sender, made the hairs on my arms stand rigid. I turned it over, scrutinizing the texture, searching for any hidden markings, any secret codes. My gaze darted around the room—the framed photos on the shelf seemed to watch me, the shadows in the corners seemed to deepen and shift with my movements. I felt a sudden, overwhelming need to dispose of it, to destroy the object before it could communicate whatever unsettling message it held. I crumpled the card into a tight, furious ball and hurled it toward the wastebasket, watching it disappear with a shaky exhale.
